name: Beaujolais Nouveau & Les Sarmentelles
tagline: At the stroke of midnight in a granite village of 2,000 souls, the first wine of the harvest gushes from the barrel and all of Beaujolais erupts.
dates: November 18–22, 2026 — midnight 'mise en perce' tapping in the night of Wed 18 to Thu 19 November (Beaujolais Nouveau Day, third Thursday, confirmed by the festival)
history: Beaujolais growers have toasted the end of harvest with barely finished wine since the 1800s, when Lyon's café owners bought barrels pressed in September and had them ready to pour by November — some famously floated down the Saône to the city's bouchons. When the Beaujolais AOC was created in 1937, the rules initially forbade sales before 15 December; they were relaxed in November 1951, when the region's wine union fixed 15 November as the official release date of what became known as Beaujolais nouveau. In 1985 the INAO standardised the date as the third Thursday of November, and to this day French law releases the wine at exactly 00:01 that morning.

What turned a rustic harvest custom into a global phenomenon was one of wine's great marketing coups. From the 1960s, races sprang up to rush the first bottles to Paris — and later London, with clubs competing by sports car, motorbike, even hot-air balloon — under the immortal slogan 'Le Beaujolais nouveau est arrivé!'. Négociant Georges Duboeuf, the 'pope of Beaujolais', pushed the frenzy across Europe in the 1980s and on to North America and Japan, where the date's early-morning arrival (thanks to time zones, Tokyo tastes it before Paris) still triggers celebrations. The wine itself is made for the moment: whole-bunch carbonic maceration of Gamay grapes, fermented in weeks, all banana-and-cherry fruit and almost no tannin.

Beaujeu, the medieval capital that gave the whole region its name, answers the hype with Les Sarmentelles — a five-day festival (its 38th edition in 2026) named for the sarments, the vine cuttings burned in a torchlit procession. Around 300 volunteers stage tastings of all twelve Beaujolais appellations, gala dinners, concerts and a wine-tasting contest whose champion traditionally wins their weight in wine. Everything builds to Wednesday midnight, when the first barrel is pierced ('la Percée') on the village square amid fireworks and cannon — the symbolic worldwide birth of the new vintage, televised internationally. After years of critics sniffing at 'banana juice', a new generation of serious growers has made the nouveau ritual genuinely cool again.
whyGo: This is the one night of the year when an entire wine region counts down to midnight like New Year's Eve, and Beaujeu is its epicentre — torchlight parade, barrel-piercing, fireworks, and free-flowing just-born wine in the village where Beaujolais began. It is joyously unpretentious: you drink the vintage hours after it becomes legal, shoulder to shoulder with the growers who made it. And it sits 30 minutes from Lyon, France's gastronomic capital, making it the easiest great wine pilgrimage on the calendar.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Genève (IC, ~2h45) → Lyon Part-Dieu (TGV/TER Léman Express corridor, ~2h), then TER Lyon → Belleville-sur-Saône (~30 min) + taxi/shuttle 13 km to Beaujeu
gettingThere.notes: Book SBB/TGV Lyria supersaver fares 2–3 months out; check the festival site for Belleville–Beaujeu shuttle options during Les Sarmentelles

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Basel → Besançon → Mâcon → Beaujeu via A36/A6 (~410 km)
gettingThere.notes: By far the most flexible for the midnight ceremony and for carting cases of nouveau home — but nominate a designated driver; French limit is 0.5‰

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Lyon Saint-Exupéry (LYS), then Rhônexpress tram to Lyon Part-Dieu + TER to Belleville-sur-Saône + taxi
gettingThere.notes: Short direct hop, but with airport transfers the train is usually just as fast and far more pleasant — fly only if fares are cheap

costs.notes: Estimates for transport from Zürich + 2–3 nights + festival. The event itself is cheap (tasting pass ~€15, evening shows €10–42, dinners €28–147 for the top gala with mise en perce seats); the driver is lodging — Beaujeu itself has a handful of rooms that sell out months ahead, so most budgets hinge on staying in Villefranche-sur-Saône, Belleville or Lyon and taxiing back after midnight. Splurge tier assumes the gala dinner, a wine-country château hotel and a Michelin detour in Lyon.

booking.label: Les Sarmentelles — official site & online ticketing
Destination Beaujolais tourism board — event page
Inter Beaujolais — official wine council, Nouveau info

weather: Mid-November in the Beaujolais hills is cold and often damp: highs 8–11°C, near-freezing after dark, with fog off the Saône and a real chance of drizzle. The midnight ceremony is entirely outdoors — pack a warm coat, hat, gloves, waterproof shell and shoes that can handle wet cobbles; a thermos layer under your tasting glass hand helps.
tips: Book the Wednesday-night gala dinner and your room the moment tickets open (early autumn) — Beaujeu has only a handful of beds and the mise en perce dinner sells out fast.
The main event is Wednesday NIGHT: arrive by evening on Nov 18, because the barrel is pierced at midnight into Thursday. Take the Thursday off; the party runs until very late.

Buy the tasting pass (~€15 with souvenir glass) and work through all twelve appellations — the crus (Morgon, Fleurie, Moulin-à-Vent) will convert anyone who thinks Beaujolais is only banana-scented nouveau.
Public transport back to Lyon stops long before midnight — either sleep in or near Beaujeu, pre-book a taxi from Belleville, or drive with a sober designated driver.

Skip the marked-up nouveau in Zürich supermarkets and buy direct from village caveaux and domaine open-cellar days for a few euros a bottle — many producers around Beaujeu hold portes ouvertes all weekend.
Cash still rules at outdoor stands and caveaux; bring euros in small notes, and don't expect card terminals to survive a cold, crowded square.

localRecs.name: La Maison du Terroir Beaujolais
localRecs.note: Beaujeu's own wine-and-produce house on the main square — tastings of all crus and the festival's spiritual daytime HQ

localRecs.name: Mont Brouilly
localRecs.note: Short drive/hike above the vines with a chapel on top and the best panorama of the golden autumn Beaujolais — go before the light fades on ceremony day

localRecs.name: Auberge du Cep, Fleurie
localRecs.note: Beloved village institution 15 minutes away for coq au vin-style Beaujolais cooking with serious cru lists

localRecs.name: Vaux-en-Beaujolais (Clochemerle)
localRecs.note: The village that inspired the satirical novel Clochemerle — tiny square, tasting caveau, and open-cellar nouveau weekends

localRecs.name: Les Halles de Lyon Paul Bocuse
localRecs.note: Bookend the trip in Lyon's covered food hall — oysters, quenelles and a glass of nouveau where the Lyonnais have always drunk it

photos.caption: Beaujolais nouveau celebrations in Lyon, the wine's historic first market, on release day 2025
Place des Sires-de-Beaujeu in the heart of Beaujeu, the medieval capital of Beaujolais where Les Sarmentelles takes over the streets
Open-cellar tasting of the new vintage at a domaine in Vaux-en-Beaujolais during Beaujolais nouveau weekend
